title: Flu Season

jokes.text: Except no one dresses up like a janitor when they want to be slutty.
jokes.setup: April sarcastically suggests nurses and janitors are interchangeable
jokes.punchline: Ann's dry comeback about sexy Halloween costumes
jokes.explanation: Sharp, unexpected comeback with perfect timing. The shift from professional defense to absurd cultural observation is well-crafted.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yeah, because nurses have the same training as janitors. That's why there are so many sexy janitor costumes at Halloween.

jokes.text: April's reaction beat - silence after claiming she can't do anything because she's sick
jokes.setup: April claims illness prevents her from being vindictive
jokes.punchline: The buzzing sound suggests she immediately does something petty
jokes.explanation: Perfect character moment - April's vindictive nature can't be contained even when genuinely sick. The immediate contradiction is beautifully executed.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I'm too sick to be vindictive right now.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: *buzzing sound of her using a motorized scooter to run over someone's foot*

jokes.text: I've already vomited, like, five times today.
jokes.setup: JJ downplays his illness as just allergies and tiredness
jokes.punchline: Casually mentions extreme symptom while maintaining it's minor
jokes.explanation: Classic denial-of-reality comedy. The casual delivery of extreme information creates perfect cognitive dissonance.

dialogue.line: I cannot reliably identify the speaker(s) for this joke. 'JJ' is not a main character in Parks and Recreation, and the punchline description is too vague to reconstruct specific dialogue without the actual text. Please provide the full joke text.

jokes.text: You spent over $1,000 last year on waffles alone.
jokes.setup: Leslie playfully suggests JJ says 'favorite customer' to everyone
jokes.punchline: Specific, absurd statistic proving Leslie genuinely is his favorite
jokes.explanation: Perfect Leslie characterization - her waffle obsession quantified in an absurdly specific way that's both believable and ridiculous.

dialogue.speaker: JJ
dialogue.line: You're right, I do say that to everyone. But the statistics don't lie — you're the only one who's ever bought a whole case of whipped cream at 3 AM, returned it unopened the next morning, and then came back to buy it again two weeks later.

jokes.text: I didn't know what to bring you, so I just got some magazines and lipstick. Woman stuff.
jokes.setup: Ron admits he's bad at hospital visits
jokes.punchline: His hilariously reductive approach to 'appropriate' gifts
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron Swanson moment - his masculine confusion about women expressed in the most awkwardly sweet way possible.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm not good at hospital visits. I don't know what to say to people who are sick.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: So I usually just bring them a get-well gift.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Last time I visited someone in the hospital, I brought them a bottle of scotch and a bottle of bacon grease.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I thought, 'Well, if they're stuck in bed, they might as well enjoy the two best things in life.'

jokes.text: I once worked with a guy for three years and never learned his name.
jokes.setup: Ron explains his philosophy about not caring about people
jokes.punchline: Extreme example of his emotional detachment
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron philosophy taken to absurd extremes. The matter-of-fact delivery makes the antisocial behavior even funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have no political beliefs. And, by the way, I don't care about people.

jokes.text: Best friend I ever had. We still never talk sometimes.
jokes.setup: Ron's story about not learning a coworker's name
jokes.punchline: The paradoxical nature of their 'friendship' and ongoing non-communication
jokes.explanation: Brilliant escalation that perfectly captures Ron's worldview. The phrase 'never talk sometimes' is comedic gold - a logical impossibility that makes perfect sense for the character.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I have been working with this man for years, and I still don't know his name. Every time I almost learn it, something happens and I have to start all over again.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: At this point, asking would be admitting I don't know. So we have this unspoken agreement: I pretend to know him, he pretends I know him, and we both go about our business.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: It's actually the most honest friendship I have.

jokes.text: You look tired and you're all sweaty all the time. What's your excuse?
jokes.setup: Jerry comments that Leslie looks tired and sweaty
jokes.punchline: Leslie's instant, brutal comeback about Jerry's general appearance
jokes.explanation: Sharp comeback that establishes the Jerry-abuse dynamic. Leslie's fever making her more brutal than usual adds character depth.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, I'm tired and sweaty because I've been working hard. You look like that on your best day.

jokes.text: Ended with a five-and-a-half minute mile. My personal low.
jokes.setup: Ann asks about Chris's run
jokes.punchline: Impossibly fast mile time treated as disappointing
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris characterization - his superhuman fitness expectations. The impossibility of the time makes his disappointment absurd.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: How was your run?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I did a mile in 4 minutes and 23 seconds.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Chris, that's incredible!

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I know. I'm disappointed. My goal was 4:20.

jokes.text: I think the pavement in this town is soft.
jokes.setup: Chris explains his 'slow' mile time
jokes.punchline: Blames external factors for impossible performance
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character detail - Chris's need to rationalize any perceived failure, even when the 'failure' is superhuman performance.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: My mile time was slow, but that's only because I was running into a headwind. And the track was slightly uphill. And I hadn't eaten in six hours. And I was wearing jeans.

jokes.text: My body's like a chip, too. A potato chip.
jokes.setup: Chris compares his body to a finely-tuned microchip
jokes.punchline: Ann's immediate self-deprecating comparison
jokes.explanation: Perfect comedic structure - takes Chris's pretentious metaphor and deflates it with relatable self-awareness. Economy of language is excellent.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Yeah, well, my body is more like a microwave that someone dropped down the stairs.

jokes.text: There was one time I thought he farted. But it was me.
jokes.setup: Ann explains she can't find any flaws in Chris
jokes.punchline: Embarrassing personal revelation about their relationship
jokes.explanation: Classic awkward overshare that reveals both Ann's search for flaws and her tendency toward TMI confessions.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I literally cannot find a single flaw in Chris. He's perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Which is weird because usually I can find something wrong with everyone. But with Chris, there's nothing.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Except... he won't let me organize his sock drawer. He says it's 'fine as is.'

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: And honestly? That might be the hottest thing he does.

jokes.text: April really is the whole package.
jokes.setup: Ron needs someone to replace April who is mean and apathetic
jokes.punchline: Describing negative qualities as 'the whole package'
jokes.explanation: Ron's appreciation for April's hostility expressed as if describing positive attributes. Perfect character consistency.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I need someone to replace April. Someone who's mean, apathetic, has no respect for authority, and actively works against the department's interests.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: So you're looking for another April?

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: No. I'm looking for someone with all of those qualities, plus they have to be good at their job. April was the whole package.

jokes.text: Check this out. I just invented it. Super-straw.
jokes.setup: Andy announces he has something new to show
jokes.punchline: His 'invention' is just straws taped together
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - childlike excitement over simple concept he thinks is revolutionary. Visual gag enhances the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, I have something new to show you guys.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I call it... the Mega Straw. It's just straws taped together.

jokes.text: I have 2.8% body fat. My body is like a microchip. A grain of sand could destroy it.
jokes.setup: Chris realizes Leslie is sick
jokes.punchline: Extreme description of his vulnerability despite apparent health
jokes.explanation: Escalates the microchip metaphor to absurd extremes. Chris's health obsession makes his peak fitness a vulnerability.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Leslie, you're sick? But you're so healthy and strong. I've never seen you vulnerable before. It's actually kind of... attractive? No, wait, that's wrong. I mean, I'm concerned about your wellbeing as a colleague and friend.

jokes.text: My body's a microchip.
jokes.setup: Chris just used the microchip metaphor
jokes.punchline: Feverish Leslie immediately copies his pretentious language
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that shows Leslie's fever making her susceptible to Chris's ridiculous metaphors.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Yes! A microchip! That's exactly what I was going to say. We need to think of Pawnee as a microchip, and each department is like a tiny transistor, and if one transistor fails, the whole microchip... wait, what does a microchip do?

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: It processes information.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Right, right. So we need to process information better. As a microchip.

jokes.text: I have to get ready for the Chamber of Secrets. Commerce.
jokes.setup: Leslie trying to explain her important meeting
jokes.punchline: Fever causes Harry Potter mix-up with quick correction
jokes.explanation: Simple fever-induced malapropism. The quick self-correction adds to the comedy by showing Leslie's awareness.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I have a very important meeting with the city council about the Ramsett Park expansion project.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I've been preparing for weeks, going over all the details, making sure everything is perfect.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is basically like when Harry Potter had to prepare for the Triwizard Tournament.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I mean, not Harry Potter. I don't know why I said that. I have a fever.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: This is like when I had to prepare to become Deputy Director.

jokes.text: Leslie, I typed your symptoms into the thing up here, and it says you could have 'network connectivity problems'.
jokes.setup: Andy offers to help diagnose Leslie's condition
jokes.punchline: He's clearly using a computer error message as medical diagnosis
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - his technological ineptitude combined with earnest desire to help creates absurd but endearing result.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Okay, so what are your symptoms?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I've been feeling dizzy and nauseous all morning.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hmm, let me see... Have you tried turning it off and turning it back on again?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: What? That's not medical advice.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No wait, wait. I got it. You have a fatal error. You need to restart your system immediately.

jokes.text: If I was sick, could I do this? [attempts cartwheels] What are you doing? Cartwheels. Am I not doing them? No.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to prove she's not sick
jokes.punchline: Her attempted cartwheels are so bad Ann has to ask what she's doing
jokes.explanation: Classic physical comedy enhanced by dialogue. Leslie's complete lack of awareness of her failure makes it funnier.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm not sick, see? I can do a cartwheel!

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What are you doing?

jokes.text: It's not that I don't trust Ben. It's that I don't have faith in Ben. And, also, I'm starting to forget who Ben is.
jokes.setup: Leslie explains her concerns about Ben handling the presentation
jokes.punchline: Escalating from professional doubt to fever-induced memory loss
jokes.explanation: Perfect escalation that moves from reasonable concern to absurd fever logic. The precise distinction between trust and faith is oddly philosophical.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm just worried that Ben might not be ready for this presentation. He's been working so hard, but what if he freezes up? What if he forgets his talking points? What if he gets a fever and forgets who he is?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, I'll be fine. It's just a presentation.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: That's what you said before the Harvest Festival, and you ended up with heat exhaustion.

jokes.text: I try to be considerate. Well, I am off for a soak and a schvitz. Arrivederch.
jokes.setup: Tom claims he tries to be considerate while abandoning Ben
jokes.punchline: Immediately leaves for spa while mispronouncing 'arrivederci'
jokes.explanation: Classic Tom selfishness disguised as consideration. The mispronunciation adds character depth - trying to be sophisticated but failing.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: I'm going to the spa. Arrivaderci!

jokes.text: I just dropped another call. [DIAL TONE]
jokes.setup: Andy is filling in as receptionist
jokes.punchline: Immediately demonstrates his incompetence
jokes.explanation: Simple character consistency joke. Andy's matter-of-fact announcement of failure is endearing rather than frustrating.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey there! Welcome to Parks and Rec. I'm Andy, I'm the receptionist now.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: So what can I do you for? Wait, that's not right. What can I do for you?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, I don't know where anything is. Or who anyone is. Is this the Parks Department or the... um... Rec Department?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You know what, let me transfer you to someone who might know what they're doing.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: How do I do that? Do I just yell into the phone?

jokes.text: Help! This slutty nurse is trying to smother me to death with a pillow! Stay back, slut!
jokes.setup: Ann brings April fresh pillows
jokes.punchline: April's dramatic overreaction treating kindness as murder attempt
jokes.explanation: Perfect April vindictiveness. The callback to 'slutty nurse' shows her commitment to being awful even when sick.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I brought you some fresh pillows.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Why would you do this? What did I ever do to you?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Are you trying to kill me? Is this poisoned?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I trusted you, Ann. I thought we were friends.

jokes.text: I'm going to go into a supply closet and snap a bunch of tongue depressors.
jokes.setup: Ann explains she'll maintain professionalism
jokes.punchline: Her specific, oddly violent stress relief plan
jokes.explanation: Unexpected glimpse into Ann's coping mechanisms. The specificity of 'tongue depressors' makes it funnier than generic stress relief.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm going to maintain professionalism and keep my emotions in check.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I've been stress testing a new technique where I imagine Ron's face on a punching bag, then I actually punch a real bag, but I'm picturing his face.

jokes.text: You're too white.
jokes.setup: Leslie offers to write Ben a rap for his presentation
jokes.punchline: Immediately rejects her own idea with blunt assessment
jokes.explanation: Leslie's fever-induced bluntness creates unexpected honesty. The immediate self-contradiction is character-consistent.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I could write you a rap for your presentation!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Actually, no. That's a terrible idea. I'm not a rapper.

jokes.text: I am starving. I haven't had lunch since yesterday.
jokes.setup: Ron expresses hunger
jokes.punchline: Treats missing one meal as extreme starvation
jokes.explanation: Classic Ron exaggeration about food. His masculine dramatics over minor inconvenience fits character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm hungry.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I haven't eaten since breakfast. I'm basically starving to death.

jokes.text: It literally killed a guy last year.
jokes.setup: Andy recommends the 'Meat Tornado' burrito
jokes.punchline: Casual mention of fatal food incident as selling point
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy logic - treating death as a positive restaurant review. The casual delivery makes the dark humor work.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: You gotta try the Meat Tornado. It's amazing. A guy died eating one last year, but that's just because he didn't appreciate it enough.

jokes.text: You had me at 'Meat Tornado'.
jokes.setup: Andy's description of dangerous burrito
jokes.punchline: Ron is sold immediately on the name alone
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron response - the ridiculous meat-centric name is all he needs to hear. Ignores the death aspect entirely.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: It's called the Pit. It comes in a bag. You eat it with a shovel.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: I'm in.

jokes.text: Before I joined, they were just three old white dudes quietly boiling in hot water.
jokes.setup: Tom explains his value to the spa group
jokes.punchline: Describes spa as cooking metaphor with racial awareness
jokes.explanation: Tom's self-importance combined with accurate description of boring rich guys. The 'boiling' metaphor adds visual humor.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: A spa is like a kitchen, right? You got your chef, that's me. You got your sous chefs, that's you guys. And you got your ingredients. But here's the thing - I'm not gonna use the word 'ingredients' because that's a little too close to how people used to talk about, you know, other people. So let's call them 'components.' I'm the chef, you're my sous chefs, and this spa experience is the dish we're cooking up together.

jokes.text: Forgot to mention, the tiny kangaroo is a racist.
jokes.setup: Tom's 'would you rather' game about pocket kangaroos
jokes.punchline: Adds completely unexpected racist kangaroo twist
jokes.explanation: Completely unexpected escalation that takes silly hypothetical into dark territory. The absurdity of a racist kangaroo is memorable.

jokes.text: Not like, 'Damn, you're fine,' but fine health-wise.
jokes.setup: Andy asks Ann about her sudden illness
jokes.punchline: Clarifies his use of 'fine' to avoid accidental flirtation
jokes.explanation: Andy's social awareness preventing misunderstanding. Sweet character moment showing his growth and loyalty to April.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Hey Ann, are you okay? You look sick.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: I'm fine.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: No, I mean you actually look sick. I'm not trying to flirt with you or anything. When I say 'fine,' I mean actually fine, not the thing where guys say a girl is fine.

jokes.text: The microchip has been compromised.
jokes.setup: Chris realizes he has the flu
jokes.punchline: Maintains the absurd microchip metaphor while sick
jokes.explanation: Chris maintains his pretentious metaphor even while experiencing the thing he feared. Character consistency through crisis.

jokes.text: Pierre Garcon was a sixth-round pick. Collie was the fourth round. Indianapolis Colts know how to draft so well.
jokes.setup: Chris is delirious with fever
jokes.punchline: Random football statistics as fever-induced rambling
jokes.explanation: Chris's perfect health obsession extends to sports knowledge. Fever doesn't stop his need to demonstrate expertise.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Did you know that in 1987, the Denver Broncos had the best defense in the NFL? Forty-four sacks in a single season. Forty-four! That's like... that's like one sack for every... *coughs* ...I don't know, math is hard when you're sick. But the point is, John Elway threw for 3,198 yards that year, which is less than I thought, but more than I have fingers and toes combined. Also, the average NFL player makes $1.9 million a year, but that was in 2010, so now it's probably more, or less, depending on inflation and other economic factors that I'm too feverish to understand right now.

jokes.text: You are an unstoppable good idea machine!
jokes.setup: Andy suggests running pass plays after eating
jokes.punchline: Ron's enthusiastic praise for simple suggestion
jokes.explanation: Ron's appreciation for Andy's simple masculinity. The over-the-top praise for basic suggestion shows their bonding.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: We should run some pass plays after we eat.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: That's the best idea you've ever had.

jokes.text: I'm surrounded by a lot of women in this department. And that includes the men.
jokes.setup: Ron explains why he likes Andy
jokes.punchline: Brutal assessment of his male coworkers' masculinity
jokes.explanation: Perfect Ron philosophy about masculinity. The pause before 'and that includes the men' builds the comedic impact perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: Ron
dialogue.line: Andy's the only one of you idiots who actually knows how to do anything. Ben's too uptight. Jerry's too stupid. Tom's too greedy. And Leslie... well, Leslie's a woman.

jokes.text: Leslie came in here and stole my flu medicine and left.
jokes.setup: April claims she needs more flu medicine
jokes.punchline: Reveals Leslie's drug theft in matter-of-fact way
jokes.explanation: Sets up Leslie's drug-fueled presentation. April's casual reporting of theft fits her character perfectly.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: I need more flu medicine.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: April, I already gave you the whole bottle.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Yeah, and you drank half of it.

jokes.text: I had a dream that she came into this room, stole my flu medicine, told me not to tell you, and then disappeared through that hole in the wall. The door?
jokes.setup: April describes Leslie's visit as a dream
jokes.punchline: Ann's practical correction about the 'hole in the wall'
jokes.explanation: April's creative truth-telling combined with Ann's literal-mindedness. The 'door' correction is perfect straight-man work.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Leslie came to my house last night. It was like a dream.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: April, that literally was a dream. You were asleep.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: No, it was real. She came through a hole in the wall.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: That's your closet door, April.

jokes.text: I looked at the meter, and it had Egyptian hieroglyphics on it.
jokes.setup: Leslie asks Ben for cab money
jokes.punchline: Fever makes her see hieroglyphics on taxi meter
jokes.explanation: Creative fever hallucination that's specific and visual. Shows Leslie's condition while maintaining her earnest delivery.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, can you lend me some cab money?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Sure, here you go.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Thanks. I'm running a fever and I'm seeing hieroglyphics on the taxi meter.

jokes.text: The floor and the wall just switched.
jokes.setup: Ben warns Leslie to be careful walking
jokes.punchline: Leslie's matter-of-fact description of impossible spatial shift
jokes.explanation: Simple but effective fever logic. Leslie treats impossible thing as minor inconvenience worth mentioning.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Be careful walking down those stairs.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Don't worry, I'm great at spatial reasoning. I can walk down stairs forwards, backwards, sideways. I once walked down a flight of stairs while doing a cartwheel.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That's not possible.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I landed on my head, but I did it.

jokes.text: Good evening, everyone. I'm Leslie Monster. And this is Nightline.
jokes.setup: Leslie begins her important presentation
jokes.punchline: Opens with wrong name and wrong show format
jokes.explanation: Perfect setup for disaster presentation. 'Leslie Monster' is fever-induced but oddly appropriate for her condition.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Good morning everyone, I'm Leslie Knope, and welcome to 'The Real Housewives of Pawnee.'

jokes.text: Holy... [BLEEP] I can remember things.
jokes.setup: Leslie correctly answers political question
jokes.punchline: Her surprise at basic cognitive function
jokes.explanation: Leslie's genuine amazement at remembering things shows how far her fever has progressed. The profanity adds impact.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Wait, I got that right? I didn't even study!

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I just assumed all the answers were 'America' and one of them had to be correct.

jokes.text: I vomited somewhere in this room. I don't remember where, though.
jokes.setup: Leslie is having dinner with business owners
jokes.punchline: Casual admission of vomiting with uncertain location
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment - information nobody wants delivered with Leslie's typical helpful transparency.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I'm so excited about this business opportunity. I might have thrown up a little in my mouth just now, but I'm not sure if it was in my mouth or on the table.

jokes.text: Stop pooping.
jokes.setup: Leslie is sick and delirious
jokes.punchline: Random command to her own body
jokes.explanation: Simple fever-induced loss of filter. The directness and randomness make it memorably uncomfortable.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Kidneys, you're on notice. Liver, you're next.

jokes.text: This floor is my friend.
jokes.setup: Ann tries to get Leslie up from the floor
jokes.punchline: Leslie's emotional attachment to the floor
jokes.explanation: Simple fever logic that treats inanimate object as companion. Leslie's earnest delivery sells the absurdity.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: Leslie, come on, get up.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: I can't. I'm emotionally attached to this floor now. We've been through so much together.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: You've been lying there for twenty minutes.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Exactly. That's a lifetime in floor years.

jokes.text: Nothing like a complete physical breakdown to make a guy seem less intimidating. I love the flu.
jokes.setup: Chris observing his own illness
jokes.punchline: Finding positive aspects of being completely incapacitated
jokes.explanation: Perfect Chris moment - finding the silver lining in complete physical failure. Shows his relentless optimism even when delirious.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: You know, being completely incapacitated has really given me time to reflect on the positive aspects of my life.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I've realized that I don't have to go to the gym because I literally cannot move.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: And I don't have to meal prep because I can only consume liquids.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: So really, this is the most optimized I've ever been.

jokes.text: Hey, this ain't your call, McCluskey.
jokes.setup: Ben tries to stop Leslie from presenting
jokes.punchline: Leslie calls him a random Irish name
jokes.explanation: Random fever-induced name calling. The tough-guy delivery with wrong name creates mild confusion and humor.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, you can't present this to the council.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Oh, come on, Ben! Why not?

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Because it's not ready. We need more time.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Listen here, Seamus, I'm presenting this today.

jokes.text: Every Thursday night is ladies' night down at the Snakehole Lounge over on Burnham Avenue. Ladies get two drinks for the price of one. Oh, no, that can't be right. That's way too good of a deal. Nope. That is what it says. Wow.
jokes.setup: Tom announces before important presentation
jokes.punchline: Gets distracted by his own promotion's impossibly good deal
jokes.explanation: Perfect Tom moment - inappropriate business plug that he then questions. Shows his entrepreneurial nature and complete lack of business sense.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Okay everyone, I have a very important presentation about the budget crisis, but first I want to tell you about this amazing deal I just got on a leather briefcase. It's half off at Nordstrom. Half off! Do you know what this means? It means I'm basically making money by buying it.

dialogue.speaker: Tom
dialogue.line: Wait, that's not right. Let me think about this math again. Actually, I think I need to buy two briefcases now. I'm going to run down to Nordstrom real quick.

jokes.text: What the [BLEEP] is your problem? Whoa, I thought you weren't gonna lose it. While I was on duty, I didn't. Now it's just me.
jokes.setup: Ann confronts April about her behavior
jokes.punchline: April's precise distinction between professional and personal April
jokes.explanation: Brilliant character moment showing April's hidden professionalism. The careful distinction between work-April and real-April adds depth.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: April, you've been really rude to me all week.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: That's not true. I've been professional. It's not my fault you can't tell the difference between professional April and personal April.

dialogue.speaker: Ann
dialogue.line: What's the difference?

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Professional April wouldn't say this, but personal April thinks you're an idiot.

jokes.text: That's the most I've ever liked Ann.
jokes.setup: Ann stands up to April with passion
jokes.punchline: April's grudging respect for Ann's backbone
jokes.explanation: Perfect character growth moment. April's admission of respect shows the confrontation worked and adds depth to their relationship.

dialogue.speaker: April
dialogue.line: Okay, I respect that.

jokes.text: That was a flu-ridden Michael Jordan at the '97 NBA finals.
jokes.setup: Leslie successfully completes her presentation despite being sick
jokes.punchline: Ben's sports analogy comparing Leslie to legendary 'flu game'
jokes.explanation: Solid sports reference that elevates Leslie's achievement. Ben's admiration comes through in his choice of legendary comparison.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: That was incredible. You just pulled off the flu game. Michael Jordan played the Finals with the flu. You just presented the Sweetums deal while actually having the flu.

jokes.text: why is half of your face all swirly?
jokes.setup: Business owner asks legitimate question about sales tax
jokes.punchline: Leslie's fever makes her see facial distortions
jokes.explanation: Perfect cringe moment where Leslie's hallucinations meet public speaking. The specific 'swirly' description is memorably weird.

dialogue.speaker: Business Owner
dialogue.line: So how exactly does the sales tax work?

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, it's simple. The sales tax is calculated on the total purchase price, and then... wait, why is your face doing that? Your mouth is getting bigger. Are you a snake? Oh my God, you're a snake person!

jokes.text: Give it up, everybody, for Scott Bakula from Quantum Leap.
jokes.setup: Leslie tries to exit gracefully from presentation
jokes.punchline: Introduces Ben as random 90s TV actor
jokes.explanation: Random pop culture reference that shows Leslie's fever-addled brain. Scott Bakula is specific enough to be funny rather than generic.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, I think that covers everything. Thank you all for coming. Before I go, I'd like to introduce our special guest speaker.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Please welcome Ben Wyatt, star of 'Lil' Sebastian: The Reckoning.'

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: I was never in that show.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Well, you are now!

jokes.text: will you have Lady Pennyface retrieve it and send it post-hence?
jokes.setup: Leslie asks Ben to find her tiara
jokes.punchline: Makes up fancy name and uses 'post-hence' instead of proper word
jokes.explanation: Fever-induced fancy language that sounds sophisticated but makes no sense. Shows Leslie trying to maintain dignity while delirious.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Ben, I need you to find my tiara. It's very important to me.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Leslie, it's just a tiara.

dialogue.speaker: Leslie
dialogue.line: Just a tiara? This is the Countess Sparklington's crown of majesty, and I need it post-hence!

jokes.text: You could be asleep for hours. Maybe I'll come back later.
jokes.setup: Andy promises to stay by April's side while she sleeps
jokes.punchline: Immediately changes his mind about the time commitment
jokes.explanation: Perfect Andy moment - genuine love combined with realistic attention span. The immediate contradiction is endearingly honest.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: I'm gonna stay right here by your side while you sleep. I'm not going anywhere.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: Actually, you know what? I'm gonna go grab some lunch. I'll be back in like... four hours.

jokes.text: Gross. Your forehead is all sweaty. That's gross. But I still like you.
jokes.setup: Andy tries to be romantic with sleeping April
jokes.punchline: Honest disgust at her fever symptoms followed by loyalty affirmation
jokes.explanation: Sweet Andy moment showing his inability to filter thoughts. The honesty about grossness followed by affection is genuinely touching.

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: April, you're so beautiful when you sleep. Your skin is so soft and your hair smells like... oh my god, what is that smell? Did something die in your mouth? Have you been licking a tire?

dialogue.speaker: Andy
dialogue.line: But I love you anyway.

jokes.text: Way to go, buddy. Way to go.
jokes.setup: Chris mentions going for a light run after being hospitalized
jokes.punchline: His catchphrase delivered while still recovering from flu
jokes.explanation: Simple callback that shows Chris's return to form. The repetition of self-encouragement is character-consistent but not particularly clever.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: I'm going to go for a light run. I'm feeling much better.

dialogue.speaker: Ben
dialogue.line: Chris, you were just hospitalized with the flu.

dialogue.speaker: Chris
dialogue.line: Treat yo self.

tmdb_overview: While planning for the local Harvest Festival, Leslie is struck with the flu and is forced to go to the hospital. Ron and Andy have a day of male bonding, and Tom hits the spa.
insight: Flu season spreads 56 jokes across 22 minutes—Parks' densest episode yet through pure character chaos.
